text: The surgeon enters the lesser sac and divides the short gastric vessels using a vessel sealer. The liver is retracted with a Penrose drain, and a 3-0 Vicryl suture is placed on the posterior stomach for external retraction, exposing the splenic hilum. The posterior stomach is dissected, and attention shifts to the inferior pancreas and spleen. The splenocleidomastoid ligament is divided, the inferior border of the pancreas is defined and dissected, and the mental branch from the splenic artery is divided. An additional suture is placed on the posterior stomach to expose the pancreatic neck.

The surgeon uses Mehron bipolar forceps to expose the middle colic vein towards the superior mesenteric vein, carefully dissecting small pancreatic branches. The superior pancreas is dissected, and lymph nodes are removed from the common hepatic artery. The pancreas is then tunneled and divided using a surgical stapler.

The surgeon carefully dissects and ligates the dorsal pancreatic artery at the root of the splenic artery. The splenic artery is encircled, clamped, and confirmed to preserve hepatic artery flow using fluorescent angiography. The splenic artery is then clipped and divided, followed by division of the splenic vein using a stapler.

The surgeon dissects the deeper layer of the retroperitoneum behind the splenic artery to sample lymph nodes, exposing the aorta and left adrenal vein. The dissection follows the retroperitoneal fascial plane, and a 19 French Blake drain is placed at the end of the procedure.
